coredata.dc:title: One arrow two hawk approach for simultaneous detection and photoreduction of toxic hexavalent chromium by N, S-co-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Laddha, Harshita
Yadav, Priya
Sharma, Priya
Agarwal, Madhu
Gupta, Ragini
coredata.dc:description: The pervasive presence of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) ions in water, coupled with their highly toxic and carcinogenic nature, poses a serious threat to aquatic ecosystems and human well-being. Hence, to mitigate this concern, blue luminescent carbon dots (CG-CDs) have been synthesized using readily available low-cost precursors, namely citric acid and glutathione, which exhibited remarkable capabilities for both sensing as well as photoreduction of toxic Cr(VI). Their selectivity and sensitivity are truly remarkable, with a detection limit as low as 78 nM showing no significant interference with other ions. In addition to its application in fluorometric detection, CG-CDs also showed impressive photocatalytic properties for the reduction of hazardous Cr(VI) to less toxic Cr(III) ions when exposed to sunlight, achieving ∼ 99 % conversion of 25 ppm Cr(VI) within a mere 30 min at pH 4 using 1 mg/mL of CG-CDs. Additionally, the effective sensing and reduction ability of CG-CDs towards Cr(VI) in real water samples makes them versatile material for detecting and photoreducing Cr(VI) in aqueous environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chromium(VI)
Doped carbon dots
Fluorescent
Detection
Photoreduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen, sulphur doped carbon dots (CG-CDs)
Fluorescence sensing of chromium(VI) by CG-CDs
Photoreduction studies of chromium(VI) by CG-CDs
Structural and physicochemical characterization of CG-CDs
Fluorescence sensing studies using CG-CDs for the detection of chromium(VI)
Photocatalytic studies of CG-CDs for the reduction of Cr(VI)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrographs of CG-CDs at (a) 50 nm; (b) 10 nm; and (c) Histogram for size distribution of CG-CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of CG-CDs with their percent elemental composition; The high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s; (c) O 1s; (d) N 1s; (e) S 2p; (f) FTIR spectrum of CG-CDs.
(a) Absorption spectrum in inset with the visual photographs of CG-CDs under UV and daylight; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CG-CDs excited at various wavelengths from 280 to 400 nm.

(a) FL spectra of CG-CDs with various metal ions (150 μM) at λ ex: 340 nm; (b) FL titration plot of CG-CDs at varying concentrations (0 – 550 µM) of hexavalent chromium; (c) Stern-Volmer plot of quenching of CG-CDs emission intensity at 420 nm by Cr(VI); (d) Photographic image of the CG-CDs with different metal ions under UV-light illumination.

(a) FL emission and excitation spectra of CG-CDs with UV–vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI) and CG-CDs and; (b) Influence on the fluorescence intensity of CG-CDs with various metal ions along with hexavalent chromium.

(a) Effect of CG-CDs on the reduction of Cr(VI) under dark and sunlight conditions; (b) Impact of different amounts of photocatalyst loading on photoreduction of Cr(VI); (c) Effect of CG-CDs (1 mg/mL) on the various concentrations of Cr(VI); (d) Kinetics plot between ln(Co/C) vs. time for the photoreduction of varying concentration Cr(VI).

(a) Impact of various types of scavengers on the hexavalent chromium ion reduction; (b) Plausible mechanism involved in the photocatalytic reduction process; (c) Photographic image for the reduction of hexavalent chromium by DPC assay.
Pictorial depiction for the synthesis of CG-CDs using citric acid and glutathione.

head.ce:title: One arrow two hawk approach for simultaneous detection and photoreduction of toxic hexavalent chromium by N, S-co-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Malaviya National Institute of Technology, Jaipur 302017, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Malaviya National Institute of Technology, Jaipur 302017, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Malaviya National Institute of Technology, Jaipur 302017, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Malaviya National Institute of Technology, Jaipur 302017, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Research Centre, Malaviya National Institute of Technology, Jaipur 302017, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Research Centre, Malaviya National Institute of Technology, Jaipur 302017, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pervasive presence of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) ions in water, coupled with their highly toxic and carcinogenic nature, poses a serious threat to aquatic ecosystems and human well-being. Hence, to mitigate this concern, blue luminescent carbon dots (CG-CDs) have been synthesized using readily available low-cost precursors, namely citric acid and glutathione, which exhibited remarkable capabilities for both sensing as well as photoreduction of toxic Cr(VI). Their selectivity and sensitivity are truly remarkable, with a detection limit as low as 78 nM showing no significant interference with other ions. In addition to its application in fluorometric detection, CG-CDs also showed impressive photocatalytic properties for the reduction of hazardous Cr(VI) to less toxic Cr(III) ions when exposed to sunlight, achieving ∼ 99 % conversion of 25 ppm Cr(VI) within a mere 30 min at pH 4 using 1 mg/mL of CG-CDs. Additionally, the effective sensing and reduction ability of CG-CDs towards Cr(VI) in real water samples makes them versatile material for detecting and photoreducing Cr(VI) in aqueous environments.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium (Cr) is a highly toxic, naturally occurring heavy metal found in the earth's crust and seawater and is an indispensable part of several industrial processes [11,32,47,49]. Hexavalent chromium, Cr(VI), has been identified as one of the most strategic and crucial contaminants often released into the environment by several sectors, such as electroplating, leather tanning, welding, dyeing mining, and pigment waste[1,21]). The accumulation of hexavalent chromium in organisms can result in various severe diseases, including skin irritation, respiratory issues, internal hemorrhage, and damage to the kidney and liver[31,48]. Thus, the World Health Organization (WHO) has imposed a maximum acceptable concentration of 0.05 mg L–1 for Cr(VI) in potable water [44]. Several methodologies have been devised to monitor and eliminate chromium(VI); however, the vast majority of them are limited to a single purpose, namely, the detection or removal of chromium(VI) exclusively[6,26,29,32,35]. Nonetheless, in practical applications, the identification and elimination of contaminants should be mutually reinforcing. Consequently, there is a strong inclination to develop an integrated system that can concurrently identify and eradicate chromium(VI), given that removing contaminants from the environment is just as critical as the analytical detection of contaminants[16]. Thus, it is imperative to develop a method for continuously detecting and removing Cr(VI).

Carbon dots are being extensively researched and advanced globally, primarily owing to their appealing characteristics, which include higher specific surface area, outstanding photoluminescence properties, lower toxicity, and biocompatibility compared to nanoparticles based on metals[27,28]. CDs possess unique properties that make them promising candidates for the advancement of novel materials in various fields[4,13,17,20,28,34,35]. Several methods can be employed to synthesize CDs, including laser ablation, hydrothermal and solvothermal treatments, ultrasonic treatment, microwave irradiation, electrochemical carbonization, and electrochemical oxidation[2,7,10,12,42]. With their stable and tunable emission properties, carbon dots emerge as captivating and eco-friendly fluorescence probes capable of detecting an array of target analytes[15,27]. Furthermore, CDs demonstrate a remarkable capability to enable the recombination annihilation process of electron-hole pairs produced upon light absorption[4,43]. Harnessing the dual characteristics of fluorescence and catalysis, these carbon dots present significant potential for their utilization as versatile nanomaterials exhibiting the ability to concurrently detect and photocatalytically eradicate toxic contaminants, thereby addressing the challenge of effectively integrating real-time monitoring and efficient removal of pollutants [10,36].

Thus, a simple and efficient approach to synthesize blue luminescent carbon dots (CG-CDs) using citric acid and glutathione via microwave-induced pyrolysis have been developed (Scheme 1 ). Analyses were done in order to describe the microstructural, morphological, and physicochemical properties of CG-CDs. The distinctive optical and electrical characteristics of CG-CDs make them a promising dual-purpose tool for the simultaneous detection and mitigation of carcinogenic hexavalent chromium from water. Further, detailed investigations were conducted to examine the impact of various factors on fluorescent detection and photoreduction of Cr(VI) by CG-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen, sulphur doped carbon dots (CG-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The CG-CDs were produced by following simple and rapid microwave-induced pyrolysis [24]. Briefly, 10 mmol of citric acid (CA) and 5 mmol of glutathione (GLU) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. Then, the conical containing transparent solution was placed and microwaved in the domestic oven at 600 W for 6 min, resulting in charring of precursors. After microwave charring, the yellow-colored viscous mix was diluted with 10 mL of DI and left to cool down to ambient temperature. Subsequently, the yellow-colored solution was centrifuged (11,000 rpm) for 20 min, and the obtained supernatant liquid was passed through a 0.22 µm syringe filter and further dialyzed against water to obtain pure CG-CDs which were vacuum dried and re-dispersed in water to form a homogenous solution. The carbon dots solution (2 mg/mL) was stored at 4 °C for further studies. Additionally, the amount of starting precursors utilized in the synthesis procedure was optimized against quantum yield (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of chromium(VI) by CG-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorometric sensing analysis of hexavalent chromium by CG-CDs was done using DI water as a solvent at 25 °C with 340 nm as an excitation and 420 nm as an emission wavelength. The stock solutions of several metal ions (1 mM) were prepared in DI water at pH 7. The above stock solutions were then later diluted to prepare the requisite concentrations. All fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded at fixed emission and excitation slits (3 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoreduction studies of chromium(VI) by CG-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To examine the photocatalytic activity of the CG-CDs for the reduction of Cr(VI), a stock solution of 100 ppm by mixing 0.27 g potassium dichromate in 1000 mL of DI water was prepared and later diluted. A standard procedure involved the addition of 1 mg/mL of CG-CDs into 20 mL of 25 ppm Cr(VI) solution, and the solution was placed in the dark for 25 min to attain the absorption–desorption equilibria. Afterward, the solution was placed in sunlight, and subsequently, at an interval of 5 min, a 400 μL sample was taken and centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 2 min. The progress of the reduction process was monitored at a fixed wavelength, i.e., 540 nm, after adding a standard DPC assay solution[22], which produces pink color with free Cr(VI) metal ions at different time intervals to evaluate and optimize the photocatalytic properties at various parameters. The photoreduction conversion efficiency of Cr(VI) into Cr(III) utilizing CG-CDs was calculated from UV–vis spectra following the given equation[1]:
(1)
% C o n v e r s i o n =
C
o
-
C
t
C
o
× 100
where Co is the initial concentration, and Ct is the final concentration at different intervals of time (t).
The detailed description of the materials, instrumentation, and general procedure for quantum yield measurement used in this study are provided in Text S1-S3 in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and physicochemical characterization of CG-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The HRTEM technique was utilized to determine the size and morphology of CG-CDs (Fig. 1 (a, b)), which revealed that the prepared CDs are well dispersed and spherical. The particle size distribution histogram of the CG-CDs is shown in Fig. 1 (c), with particle sizes varying from 1 to 5 nm and a mean size of around 2.98 nm.

The XPS has been applied to explore the surficial states and elemental composition of the CG-CDs. The survey spectrum of CG-CDs is presented in Fig. 2 (a), with four peaks at 285.5, 531.0, 399.5, and 163.0 eV with their percent elemental composition corresponding to C 1s (77.22 %), O 1s (17.32 %), N 1s (3.77 %) and S 2p (1.70 %)[14,19,39]. Fig. 2 (b) shows the four peaks in the deconvoluted C 1s spectra at 287.7, 285.5, 284.2, and 283.8 eV corresponding to C=N/ C=O, C−O/ C−N, C−C and C=C bonds, respectively[38]. Fig. 2 (c) displays the high-resolution spectra of O 1s with two deconvoluted peaks at 531.4 eV and 530.4 eV attributable to the O−C and O=C bonded oxygen, respectively[30,41]. In the high-resolution spectra of N 1s (Fig. 2 (d)), the peaks at 398.8, 399.5, and 400.9 eV were assigned to C−N−C (pyridinic), C−N−H (pyrrolic), and C3−N (graphitic) bindings, respectively[37]. The high-resolution spectra of S 2p shown in Fig. 2 (e) are deconvoluted into two peaks at 162.6 eV and 163.8 eV related to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively. FTIR investigation also provides an overview of the functional groups present on the CG-CDs (Fig. 2 (f)). The strong absorption band at 3433 cm−1 is due to N–H and O–H stretching vibrations[28]. A weak absorption band at 2919 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of =C−H bond. The characteristic peaks centered at 1679, 1422, and 1090 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of C=O, N−H, and C−O−C groups, respectively[23,33,40]. XPS and FTIR examinations indicated that CG-CDs are viable candidates for future application studies since they are water soluble and contain an abundance of hydrophilic groups.

An absorption spectrum of CG-CDs displays a distinct peak at ∼ 350 nm due to n–π* transitions of C=O/ C=N groups exhibiting pale yellow and blue color in ambient light and UV light illumination, respectively (Fig. 3 (a)) [45,46]. The fluorescence emission spectra of CG-CDs show excitation wavelength-independent behavior when excited from 280 nm to 400 nm. Fig. 3 (b) depicts the maximum emission obtained at 420 nm when CG-CDs were excited at 340 nm (λ max). The QY of CG-CDs taking quinine sulphate as a reference was calculated to be ∼ 70 % in the water [8,19].

The influence of pH was studied on the FL intensity of CG-CDs varying from 2 to 12. According to Fig. S2 (a), no noteworthy effect was observed on the FL intensity of CG-CDs, showing the pH-independent emission of CG-CDs due to highly stable structural morphology and dominance of –C=O and –OH functional groups. However, the slight decrease in FL intensity at high acidic and basic conditions may be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of readily ionizable –NH2 and –COOH functional groups [14,33]. The stability of the CG-CDs regarding storage, UV irradiation and ionic conditions was investigated assessing their suitability as a fluorescent probe in multiple fields. Fig. S6 displays the fluorescence spectra of CG-CDs over various storage times, ranging from 1 to 120 days. Remarkably, there is no noticeable decline in fluorescence intensity, even after 120 days, affirming the enduring stability of CG-CDs. Moreover, Fig. S2 (b) showed the FL spectra of CG-CDs with no distinct change in intensity after continuous irradiation of UV light (365 nm) for 120 min, suggesting significant photostability of the CG-CDs. Also, the relatively consistent fluorescence intensity of CG-CDs in the aqueous solutions of NaCl ranges from 0 to 1.0 M, revealing the stability of CG-CDs in extreme ionic environments (Fig. S2 (c)). Thus, CG-CDs have good time-, photo-, and ionic-stability, making them an effective luminescent probe that could be valuable for various analytical and microscopic purposes [18,25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing studies using CG-CDs for the detection of chromium(VI)
ce:section.ce:para:
The analytical performance of CG-CDs has been investigated to evaluate their suitability as a potential sensor in sensing applications. The fluorescence spectra of CG-CDs revealed that an intense emission band with maximum intensity was detected at 420 nm on excitation at 340 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the CG-CDs upon adding various types of metal ions (150 μM) viz., Al(III), Hg(II), Mg(II), Zn(II), Ba(II), Co(II), Pb(II), Na(I), Ca(II), Fe(III), Cu(II), Mn(II) except for Cr(VI) show no apparent changes in fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4 (a)). Conversely, adding Cr(VI) resulted in the significant quenching of FL emission intensity of CG-CDs at 420 nm. Fig. 4 (d) shows the image of CG-CDs with various metal ions depicting fluorescence quenching of CG-CDs with Cr(VI). Furthermore, fluorescence titration studies were carried out to ascertain the FL quenching capability of the CG-CDs toward hexavalent chromium with varying concentrations ranging from 0 to 550 µM for Cr (VI), as shown in Fig. 4 (b). A gradual drop was observed in the FL intensity of CG-CDs with increasing concentrations of hexavalent chromium. Using the Stern-Volmer equation (eq. (2), the graph among Fo/F versus concentration of hexavalent chromium (Fig. 4 (c)) revealed a linear relationship ranging from 0 to 218 µM[5,23]:
(2)
F o
F
= 1 +
K
sv
[ C r
( V I )
]
(where F & Fo denote emission intensity of CG-CDs after and before adding hexavalent chromium ion, respectively. K sv represents the slope of the Stern-Volmer curve) [19]. Moreover, the detection limit was determined to be 78 nM (0.78 × 10−7 M) for hexavalent chromium calculated using 3(σ)/Ksv (where σ represents standard deviation) [9].

Further, to explore the mechanism of quenching, the absorption spectra of a quencher (Cr(VI)) and fluorophore (CG-CDs) are plotted together with excitation as well as emission spectra of CG-CDs as displayed in Fig. 5 (a).The plot depicts the good spectral overlap between the excitation band of the fluorophore and the absorption band of the quencher, suggesting the possibility of fluorescence quenching aiding the inner filter effect (IFE) [3,9](Fig. 5 (a)). Moreover, to evaluate the selectivity of CG-CDs for detecting Cr (VI), an interference study with different metal ions (200 µM) including Al(III), Hg(II), Mg(II), Zn(II), Ba(II), Co(II), Pb(II), Na(I), Ca(II), Fe(III), Cu(II), Mn(II) (Fig. 5 (b)) and anions (200 µM) including Cl–, Br–, F–, NO2 –, NO3 –, HPO4 –, I–, AcO–, SO4 2– (Fig. S3) was done at room temperature. There is no substantial change in the FL intensity of CG-CDs with other competing ions, implying that CG-CDs can be reliably utilized as a fluorescent probe to detect chromium(VI). Furthermore, the credibility of the method was explored by recovery procedure after spiking the tap water (MNIT, Jaipur Rajasthan) and river water (Chambal river, Kota, Rajasthan) samples with different Cr(VI) ion concentrations, and the results reveal a good extent of recovery rates (92 – 104 %) exhibiting the potential accuracy for quantifying hexavalent chromium ions in real samples (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic studies of CG-CDs for the reduction of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic experiment was conducted to remediate hexavalent chromium from wastewater under various circumstances, and the analysis was performed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. Initially, the efficacy of the photocatalyst was optimized by employing natural solar light as the light and energy source, along with parallel control experiments. A 20 mL solution containing 25 ppm chromium ions was optimized by adding 1 mg/mL of CG-CDs, and the mixture was agitated in the dark for 25 min to establish adsorption–desorption equilibrium between hexavalent chromium and CG-CDs. Following that, the solution was subjected to both dark and solar light conditions, with a corresponding control solution without a catalyst was also evaluated (Fig. 6 (a)). As part of a control investigation, the effect of the starting precursors (CA, GLU, and their mixture) on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) was examined (Fig. S4 (a)). At regular intervals, 400 μL samples were withdrawn, centrifuged, and treated with DPC assay, and the absorbance at a wavelength of 540 nm was measured to track the decline in hexavalent chromium ion concentrations. The results indicated that the control experiment with no photocatalyst, with starting precursors, and hexavalent chromium with photocatalyst under dark circumstances had very little influence (∼ 19 %) on Cr(VI) reduction, as depicted in Fig. 6 (a). In contrast, efficient removal (∼ 99 %) was observed when the Cr(VI) solution was exposed to sunlight in the presence of the catalyst. This demonstrates the requirement of sunlight for CG-CDs to function as a catalyst for the reduction of Cr(VI) ions. Additional optimization investigations were conducted utilizing different dosages of CG-CDs ranging from 0.25 to 2 mg/mL to eliminate 25 ppm hexavalent chromium under continuous solar light illumination. The dosage dependency findings for the elimination of 20 ppm Cr(VI) are shown in Fig. 6 (b). The plot of C/Co vs. time shows that an increased catalyst dose leads to a higher eradication of chromium ions, likely attributable to the availability of a larger number of active sites for the reduction. A fixed dosage of 1 mg/mL of CG-CDs was utilized in subsequent studies. Moreover, the impact of pH on the photocatalytic activity of CG-CDS for the reduction of Cr(VI) was also explored at different pH values ranging from 2 to 10 (Fig. S4 (b)). As expected, the pH value has a noteworthy impact on the photocatalytic performance, and lower pH values often result in increased photocatalytic activity as there is enhanced electrostatic attraction between the catalyst and chromate ion, leading to a higher reduction of Cr(VI). In this study, all the experiments were conducted at pH 4. The experiment's findings on concentration dependency indicate a positive correlation between the concentration of hexavalent chromium and the quantity of metal ions. This phenomenon leads to an obstruction in the path of photons towards the CG-CDs. As a result, a reduced number of sites were accessible for the reduction process, leading to a decline in the reduction rate (Fig. 6 (c)). The investigation of Cr(VI) ion elimination was examined by varying hexavalent chromium concentration and subsequent representation of the resulting data as ln(Co/C) against time. The results indicate that the data conformed to the pseudo-first-order kinetic model, as demonstrated by the high regression coefficient value (R2 > 0.95) presented in Fig. 6 (d).

The findings from the optimization studies indicate that CG-CDs exhibit efficacy in eliminating hexavalent chromium ions solely in the presence of sunlight, suggesting that the removal mechanism is contingent upon the production of alternative active species. In order to obtain a deeper understanding of the active species generated, a quenching experiment was conducted to trap these active species. Four different scavengers (tert-butanol (t-BuOH), sodium salt of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (Na2EDTA), para-benzoquinone (BZQ), and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were used, and the experiment was carried out with 10 × 10–3 M of scavengers in a 25 ppm hexavalent chromium ion solution with a 20 mL control set without a scavenger. The findings indicate that the removal of Cr(VI) is predominantly facilitated by electrons (e–), as evidenced by the significant reduction in removal efficiency (∼ 60 %) upon the introduction of the electron scavenger AgNO3, which effectively traps all electrons, as depicted in Fig. 7 (a). Conversely, the remaining scavengers demonstrated a relatively lower impact on diminishing the efficiency of hexavalent chromium ion elimination (∼ 75 %). Based on these observations, a plausible mechanism for the removal of hexavalent chromium ions with CG-CDs is depicted in Fig. 7 (b). The sunlight illumination results in the excitation of electrons from the valence band of CG-CDs to the conduction band, thereby producing photoinduced charge carriers. The process of electron-hole pair recombination is impeded due to the entrapment of photo-excited electrons, which results in the formation of highly reactive proton (H+) ions from water molecules. The reduction of toxic Cr(VI) ions to predominantly Cr(III) oxidation states is achieved through the combination of H+ ions and electrons [1]. The reduction in pink color intensity of Cr(VI)-complex with DPC assay, as displayed in Fig. 7 (c), indicates solar light-induced elimination of hexavalent chromium ions to less hazardous oxidation states.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of CG-CDs was also explored in the presence of different inorganic ions that may coexist in wastewater (10 ppm), including Na+ Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl–, F–, NO3 –, and HPO4 –. The graph shown in Fig. S5 (a) suggests CG-CDs serve as an effective and efficient catalyst having minimal impact on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) in the presence of inorganic ions. Moreover, the photoreduction capability of CG-CDs was assessed on various spiked real water samples collected from different sources, viz., tap water (MNIT, Jaipur Rajasthan), river water (Chambal river, Kota, Rajasthan) and textile industrial wastewater (Sanganer, Jaipur, Rajasthan). A 25 ppm Cr(VI) concentration was introduced as an external spike into the aforementioned samples. Subsequently, following the addition of CG-CDs, the samples were subsequently exposed to sunlight for 30 min. Fig. S5 (b) displays a bar plot illustrating the photoreduction efficiency of all samples, revealing the efficient photocatalytic reduction ability (> 99 %) of CG-CDs in the real water samples.

To summarise, highly fluorescent CG-CDs with QY of ∼ 70 % were synthesized through microwave charring and employed for selective recognition and the photoreduction of carcinogenic and toxic Cr(VI) into Cr(III). These blue fluorescent CG-CDs were exploited to detect hexavalent chromium in aqueous medium through fluorescence quenching. The CG-CDs exhibit exceptional selectivity for Cr(VI) over other competing ions through a “turn-off” signal with a limit of detection as low as the value 78 nM. Furthermore, CG-CDs exhibited remarkable photoreduction performance, achieving ∼ 99 % conversion of 25 ppm Cr(VI) in a 20 mL aqueous solution to Cr (III) within 30 min under sunlight irradiation, using a catalyst dosage of 1 mg/mL. The mechanistic aspects of photocatalytic reduction were evaluated by conducting various batch experiments, kinetic investigations, and radical trapping studies. Moreover, the photoreduction efficacy of CG-CDs was tested for different real water samples, which showed excellent removal efficiency (> 99 %). Based on the obtained results, CG-CDs represent a versatile and multifunctional material for the determination and photoreduction of hexavalent chromium in aqueous systems. This work opens up new avenues for the development of innovative materials with dual functionality in addressing water pollution challenges.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: H. Laddha (Grant No. (09/964(0020)/2018-EMR-I)) and P. Sharma (Grant No. (09/964(0027)/2019-EMR-I)) acknowledges CSIR-Delhi, India for Senior Research Fellowship. All authors are thankful to MRC, MNIT Jaipur for providing characterization facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2023.10.025.
